turns.content: I'm trying to port my existing project and I'm stuck on how to handle test isolation in sqlx. My current approach just runs queries without wrapping them, so if one test inserts a row, the next test sees it even though they shouldn't share state.

That's exactly what causes flaky tests when you skip transaction boundaries. The solution is to use #[sqlx::test] which automatically creates an isolated database connection for each test function and rolls back the transaction after it finishes. This ensures every test starts with a clean slate.
Wait, does that mean I don't need to manually call begin_transaction or commit? Can I just remove all my manual DB setup code?

You can move away from manual transaction handling for basic tests, but you still need to define the test function with #[sqlx::test] and pass in your database connection. The macro handles wrapping each test call in its own isolated transaction automatically.
Okay so I just add #[sqlx::test] above my test functions? What about migrations though, how do those fit in?

Migrations run before your actual tests when you use the sqlx::test macro. The macro automatically executes pending migrations on the isolated connection first, then runs your test functions with fresh schema each time.
That's helpful but I'm still confused about how exactly the isolation works at the connection level versus transaction level. It feels like magic to me right now.

Think of it like this: each test gets its own private database instance for that test run, not just a transaction on shared data. The #[sqlx::test] macro creates a temporary connection pool specifically for your test suite, runs migrations on it once, then spins up fresh connections per test function so no test can see changes from another.
Ah okay, so if I delete my manual transaction code and just use #[sqlx::test], the tests will be isolated automatically without me writing rollback logic?

Exactly. The macro handles creating the isolated environment and rolling back any changes after each test completes, so you don't need to write explicit commit or rollback calls in your test functions anymore.

I'm building a CLI tool to parse log files, and I keep trying to run my integration tests against the database. What's the difference between using a manual transaction block in my test code versus just adding the #[sqlx::test] attribute?

The main distinction is that without #[sqlx::test], you have full control but must manually wrap every test function in a BeginBlock, execute your queries, and call CommitBlock before returning. If you forget the rollback or commit, tests can leak state into each other. With #[sqlx::test], sqlx automatically handles the BeginBlock/CommitBlock lifecycle for each #[cfg(test)] module, ensuring isolation without boilerplate.
Ah okay so I just need to add that attribute? But I thought it still required me to write explicit migration code in my tests?

Not exactly. With #[sqlx::test], you don't manually run migrations inside your test functions, but sqlx does require a separate setup function that runs once before any tests execute to apply schema changes. You can define this as a standalone #[cfg(test)] fn called setup() which uses sqlx::migrate::run to load your migrations from the migrations directory. The actual test cases then just run against the migrated database.
Wait so if I have multiple tests and they all try to insert into the same table, will they affect each other? Is there any isolation?

Yes, #[sqlx::test] provides automatic per-test transaction isolation. Each test runs in its own isolated connection with its own transaction scope. When a test finishes, the transaction is rolled back automatically unless you explicitly mark it as stable using the .stable() method on your query builder result, which prevents rollback if needed.
So I can just write normal assertions and not worry about cleanup? Like calling drop or anything?

Exactly. You don't need to call drop or manually manage connections in your test functions when #[sqlx::test] is active. The framework manages the connection pool and transaction boundaries for you, so your test code looks like normal application logic without worrying about resource cleanup.

I just finished writing the test module for my new service, and it compiles cleanly. I used #[sqlx::test] to wrap my tests, which automatically migrates the database schema before each run and rolls back transactions afterward so they stay isolated. It feels like magic, but I'm wondering if there's a more idiomatic way to handle this in Rust compared to how I've been doing it with manual migrations and explicit connection pooling in other projects.

You're already on the right track by using #[sqlx::test]; that macro is specifically designed to inject the necessary setup logic, including running automatic migrations via sqlx::migration::MigrationSource and ensuring every test runs within its own isolated transaction. This eliminates the boilerplate you'd otherwise need with manual connection management or separate migration scripts. The key benefit here is that #[sqlx::test] guarantees that each test gets a fresh schema state without needing to manually call migrate_before_each in your test harness, which keeps your tests deterministic and prevents race conditions when running them in parallel.

That sounds efficient, but I'm pushing back on the idea that this replaces manual migration scripts entirely. In my previous project with a legacy database, we had complex seed data that needed to be inserted before every test run, and #[sqlx::test] might not handle arbitrary SQL inserts automatically like it handles migrations. Is there a way to combine both without losing isolation? I'm worried about the overhead if I still need to write custom setup code for those edge cases.

You're absolutely right that #[sqlx::test] doesn't magically handle arbitrary data insertion; its primary job is wrapping the migration and transaction lifecycle. However, you can combine it with manual seed logic by defining a separate setup function that runs before the test block inside your #[sqlx::test] macro. This way, you keep isolation because #[sqlx::test] still ensures a new connection per test, but you gain flexibility to run custom SQL statements like inserting specific seed rows into tables. The overhead is minimal since this just adds a few lines of setup code before the actual test logic begins, and it doesn't require abandoning the macro's benefits.

I just finished my test function and it runs, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to structure this. I used a manual transaction block with a SqlPool and manually called begin and commit for each test case. Is there an idiomatic pattern using the #[sqlx::test] macro that would handle this automatically?

Yes, you should definitely switch to the #[sqlx::test] attribute on your function signature. This macro handles connection pooling and transaction management internally, ensuring that every test runs in its own isolated transaction. It also automatically migrates your schema before each run if needed, so you don't have to manually call migrate or roll back connections between tests.

That sounds convenient, but I'm worried about the setup overhead. Does #[sqlx::test] require me to write custom logic for opening and closing the pool? Or does it just assume my code uses a SqlPool instance correctly?

You still need to provide a SqlPool instance in your test setup function, but #[sqlx::test] takes care of wrapping that pool with the necessary transaction context. The macro ensures that each individual test gets its own database connection and rolls it back automatically after execution finishes.

I see. But I'm still confused about whether #[sqlx::test] is actually needed or if I can just use a regular async fn with manual transaction control. Is there any benefit to using the macro specifically?

The main benefits are consistency and safety. With #[sqlx::test], you get guaranteed isolation per test, automatic migration support, and proper cleanup without needing to manually manage connection lifecycles or call rollback methods explicitly.
Okay, I think I understand the benefits now. But wait, could this macro cause issues if my tests are run in parallel? Will it still maintain isolation?

Actually, #[sqlx::test] does not support running multiple tests in parallel by default because the transaction isolation is per-test and requires exclusive access to the connection. If you need concurrent test execution, you would need to manually manage separate pools or use a different testing strategy.
